questions.stem: In the biosynthetic pathway shown, thromboxane A2 is produced in platelets. What is its principal physiologic action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increases renal sodium and water excretion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhibits platelet aggregation and causes vasodilation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stimulates gastric mucus and bicarbonate secretion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Triggers bronchodilation of airway smooth muscle

explanation.summary: The schematic traces membrane phospholipids through phospholipase A2 to arachidonic acid and, via COX-1/COX-2, to PGH2 and tissue-specific prostaglandins. Thromboxane A2, generated by platelets, drives platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction; prostacyclin (PGI2) opposes it with vasodilation and inhibition of aggregation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The schematic traces membrane phospholipids through phospholipase A2 to arachidonic acid and, via COX-1/COX-2, to PGH2 and tissue-specific prostaglandins. Thromboxane A2, generated by platelets, drives platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction; prostacyclin (PGI2) opposes it with vasodilation and inhibition of aggregation.

questions.stem: This figure shows cross-sections of a vessel in three states. The bottom vessel has a markedly reduced luminal diameter. Activation of which receptor on vascular smooth muscle produces this change?

explanation.summary: Alpha1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle are Gq-coupled; activation raises intracellular Ca2+ and causes vasoconstriction (the narrowed lumen), whereas beta2 activation mediates vasodilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alpha1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle are Gq-coupled; activation raises intracellular Ca2+ and causes vasoconstriction (the narrowed lumen), whereas beta2 activation mediates vasodilation.

questions.stem: This integrated schematic is used to review the pathway that statins ultimately modulate, spanning the liver, blood, enterocyte, and intestinal lumen. Which overall physiological process does it depict?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Renal handling of sodium and water

explanation.summary: The diagram traces cholesterol and triglyceride handling across hepatic synthesis, VLDL/LDL/HDL trafficking in blood, and intestinal absorption/reabsorption of cholesterol and bile acids, i.e., integrated lipoprotein metabolism, the pathway statins act on.
explanation.whyCorrect: The diagram traces cholesterol and triglyceride handling across hepatic synthesis, VLDL/LDL/HDL trafficking in blood, and intestinal absorption/reabsorption of cholesterol and bile acids, i.e., integrated lipoprotein metabolism, the pathway statins act on.

questions.stem: This illustration compares arterial cross-sections in three states of vessel diameter. Compared with the normal vessel, the state shown in the bottom row would have what effect on systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Has no effect on either resistance or blood pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increases both systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increases resistance but lowers blood pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreases resistance but raises blood pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreases both systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure

explanation.summary: The bottom vessel shows a narrowed lumen (vasoconstriction). Reducing vessel radius sharply raises resistance (resistance is inversely proportional to the fourth power of the radius), so systemic vascular resistance climbs and, with cardiac output held constant, mean arterial blood pressure rises. This is the basis of vasopressor action.
explanation.whyCorrect: The bottom vessel shows a narrowed lumen (vasoconstriction). Reducing vessel radius sharply raises resistance (resistance is inversely proportional to the fourth power of the radius), so systemic vascular resistance climbs and, with cardiac output held constant, mean arterial blood pressure rises. This is the basis of vasopressor action.

questions.stem: This diagram maps the regulation of gastric acid secretion across the cells of the stomach wall. Which cell type is the direct target of the secreted acid-producing signals and physically expresses the H+/K+-ATPase proton pump?

explanation.summary: The parietal cell is the effector cell of gastric acid secretion; it houses the H+/K+-ATPase and responds to acetylcholine (M3), gastrin, and histamine (H2). G cells secrete gastrin, ECL cells release histamine, chief cells secrete pepsinogen, and D cells release somatostatin.
explanation.whyCorrect: The parietal cell is the effector cell of gastric acid secretion; it houses the H+/K+-ATPase and responds to acetylcholine (M3), gastrin, and histamine (H2). G cells secrete gastrin, ECL cells release histamine, chief cells secrete pepsinogen, and D cells release somatostatin.

questions.stem: This schematic organizes the adrenergic receptor family into its major branches and subtypes. To which single class of membrane receptors do ALL of the receptors depicted here belong?

explanation.summary: Every adrenergic receptor shown in this classification tree (the alpha-1, alpha-2, and beta-1/2/3 subtypes) is a G-protein coupled receptor (GPCR). These seven-transmembrane receptors act through heterotrimeric G proteins to drive downstream second-messenger signaling rather than opening ion channels directly or possessing intrinsic kinase activity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Every adrenergic receptor shown in this classification tree (the alpha-1, alpha-2, and beta-1/2/3 subtypes) is a G-protein coupled receptor (GPCR). These seven-transmembrane receptors act through heterotrimeric G proteins to drive downstream second-messenger signaling rather than opening ion channels directly or possessing intrinsic kinase activity.

questions.stem: This labeled schematic shows a motor-nerve action potential arriving at a muscle cell and triggering calcium handling across the sarcolemma, T-tubule, and internal calcium store. Blocking the L-type calcium channels depicted at the T-tubule/sarcolemma would most directly reduce which downstream event?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Potassium efflux from the muscle cell

choices.label: B
choices.text: Repolarization of the motor nerve action potential

choices.label: C
choices.text: Calcium-induced calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sodium influx through voltage-gated Na+ channels

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acetylcholine release from the motor nerve terminal

explanation.summary: The diagram depicts excitation-contraction coupling: depolarization opens L-type Ca2+ channels, and the resulting Ca2+ entry triggers further Ca2+ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum (calcium-induced calcium release), which drives contraction. Blocking these channels reduces this trigger, not the upstream nerve signaling or Na+/K+ ionic currents.
explanation.whyCorrect: The diagram depicts excitation-contraction coupling: depolarization opens L-type Ca2+ channels, and the resulting Ca2+ entry triggers further Ca2+ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum (calcium-induced calcium release), which drives contraction. Blocking these channels reduces this trigger, not the upstream nerve signaling or Na+/K+ ionic currents.

questions.stem: Based on this illustration of a cholinergic synapse, how do the antagonist molecules shown at the target cell membrane produce their pharmacologic effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They block the release of acetylcholine from the synaptic vesicles

choices.label: B
choices.text: They competitively occupy the muscarinic receptors, preventing acetylcholine from binding

choices.label: C
choices.text: They directly activate the receptors to mimic acetylcholine

choices.label: D
choices.text: They enzymatically degrade acetylcholine within the synaptic cleft

choices.label: E
choices.text: They prevent the nerve impulse from reaching the presynaptic terminal

explanation.summary: Anticholinergics are muscarinic antagonists that act by competitive inhibition, binding the receptor without activating it so that endogenous acetylcholine cannot exert its effect on the target cell.
explanation.whyCorrect: Anticholinergics are muscarinic antagonists that act by competitive inhibition, binding the receptor without activating it so that endogenous acetylcholine cannot exert its effect on the target cell.

questions.stem: The schematic contrasts the normal action of the labeled mediator on its receptor (left) with drug therapy that occupies the same receptor (right). To which receptor superfamily does the receptor depicted in this diagram belong?

explanation.summary: The diagram depicts the H1 histamine receptor, a seven-transmembrane G protein-coupled receptor. Histamine released from the activated mast cell binds this GPCR to trigger downstream effects (itching, swelling, inflammation); an H1 antagonist occupies the receptor and leaves it inactive, preventing signaling. H1 receptors are not ion channels, kinase receptors, or nuclear receptors.
explanation.whyCorrect: The diagram depicts the H1 histamine receptor, a seven-transmembrane G protein-coupled receptor. Histamine released from the activated mast cell binds this GPCR to trigger downstream effects (itching, swelling, inflammation); an H1 antagonist occupies the receptor and leaves it inactive, preventing signaling. H1 receptors are not ion channels, kinase receptors, or nuclear receptors.

questions.stem: This schematic traces the sequence of hormones and enzymes shown moving from the kidney to the lung and adrenal gland. Which physiologic system is being depicted?

explanation.summary: The diagram shows renin acting on angiotensinogen to form angiotensin I, ACE converting it to angiotensin II, and downstream aldosterone secretion, which is the classic renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) that regulates blood pressure and volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: The diagram shows renin acting on angiotensinogen to form angiotensin I, ACE converting it to angiotensin II, and downstream aldosterone secretion, which is the classic renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) that regulates blood pressure and volume.

questions.stem: Comparing the left and right glomeruli in this figure, the right one shows a dilated efferent arteriole after RAAS blockade. What is the direct consequence of losing angiotensin II-mediated efferent arteriolar tone shown here?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased sodium reabsorption in Bowman capsule

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rupture of the glomerular capillary tuft

explanation.summary: Angiotensin II normally constricts the efferent arteriole to maintain glomerular pressure and filtration. When RAAS inhibitors dilate the efferent arteriole, glomerular hydrostatic pressure and GFR fall, which can precipitate prerenal acute kidney injury in low-flow states.
explanation.whyCorrect: Angiotensin II normally constricts the efferent arteriole to maintain glomerular pressure and filtration. When RAAS inhibitors dilate the efferent arteriole, glomerular hydrostatic pressure and GFR fall, which can precipitate prerenal acute kidney injury in low-flow states.

questions.stem: This diagram of a glomerulus shows a second drug class acting on the afferent arteriole by reducing vasodilator prostaglandins, compounding the efferent effect of RAAS inhibitors. Which drug class produces the afferent effect shown?

explanation.summary: NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, constricting the afferent arteriole and reducing renal blood flow. Combined with RAAS inhibitors that dilate the efferent arteriole, this greatly increases the risk of acute kidney injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, constricting the afferent arteriole and reducing renal blood flow. Combined with RAAS inhibitors that dilate the efferent arteriole, this greatly increases the risk of acute kidney injury.

questions.stem: This plot shows a single drug's effect increasing with its concentration on a log scale, leveling off at a maximum. What type of dose-response relationship does it represent?

explanation.summary: A graded dose-response curve plots the continuous magnitude of drug effect against drug concentration for a single patient, rising sigmoidally to a maximum (Emax) with the half-maximal effect defining the EC50.
explanation.whyCorrect: A graded dose-response curve plots the continuous magnitude of drug effect against drug concentration for a single patient, rising sigmoidally to a maximum (Emax) with the half-maximal effect defining the EC50.

questions.stem: This bell-shaped frequency plot shows the number of patients responding at each dose, with the central peak (mu) marked. Which pharmacodynamic parameter does that central/median value define?

explanation.summary: A quantal dose-effect frequency curve is a normal (Gaussian) distribution; the dose at its center, where 50% of the population shows the defined all-or-none response, is the median effective dose, ED50.
explanation.whyCorrect: A quantal dose-effect frequency curve is a normal (Gaussian) distribution; the dose at its center, where 50% of the population shows the defined all-or-none response, is the median effective dose, ED50.

questions.stem: This figure shows two sigmoidal curves, one for efficacy and one for toxicity, plotted against log drug concentration. A drug whose two curves are farther apart on this axis is best described as having what property?

choices.label: C
choices.text: A wider safety margin / safer drug

explanation.summary: The greater the separation between the efficacy and toxicity curves, the larger the therapeutic index and the safer the drug, because effective and toxic doses overlap less.
explanation.whyCorrect: The greater the separation between the efficacy and toxicity curves, the larger the therapeutic index and the safer the drug, because effective and toxic doses overlap less.

questions.stem: This figure plots therapeutic, toxic, and lethal effect curves with ED50, TD50, and LD50 marked. What quantity is obtained by dividing the lethal median dose by the effective median dose shown here?

explanation.summary: The therapeutic index is the ratio of a dose producing toxicity/lethality to a dose producing the desired effect (e.g., LD50/ED50 or TD50/ED50); a larger ratio indicates a safer drug.
explanation.whyCorrect: The therapeutic index is the ratio of a dose producing toxicity/lethality to a dose producing the desired effect (e.g., LD50/ED50 or TD50/ED50); a larger ratio indicates a safer drug.

questions.stem: This schematic traces how diverse stimuli lead through arachidonic acid to a family of tissue-specific products. The product shown at the far right is generated chiefly by platelets. What is its principal physiologic action?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhibits platelet aggregation and causes vasodilation

explanation.summary: The rightmost branch product is Thromboxane A2 (TxA2), synthesized largely by platelets. TxA2 is a potent promoter of platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction, which is why aspirin's inhibition of its synthesis produces an antithrombotic effect.
explanation.whyCorrect: The rightmost branch product is Thromboxane A2 (TxA2), synthesized largely by platelets. TxA2 is a potent promoter of platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction, which is why aspirin's inhibition of its synthesis produces an antithrombotic effect.
